text: The surgeon uses hot shears in R-I, a vessel sealer in R-II, and a tip-up prograsp in R-III to begin the dissection. The hepatic flexure of the colon is visualized and mobilized first. The dissection proceeds towards the right lateral parietal wall, revealing the underlying duodenum. Careful dissection is performed around the duodenum using monopolar hot shears to avoid iatrogenic injury.

The surgeon visualizes the right branch of the middle colic vein, with the duodenum and head of the pancreas clearly visible below. Dissection of the greater omentum from the mesocolon is underway, revealing the branching pattern of the middle colic vein.

The surgeon separates the cecum from the white line of Toldt in the right iliac fossa, identifying the right-sided gonadal vessels and ureter. The dissection proceeds cranially, mobilizing the terminal ileum. The cecum and appendix are lifted, and the ileocolic pedicle is ligated using a vessel sealer. The right colic pedicle is also ligated, followed by the right branch of the middle colic pedicle. The transverse colon is lifted, and the splenic flexure is mobilized to reduce tension on the anastomosis, completing the mobilization from the terminal ileum to the splenic flexure.
